semantic_journey: Arabic الطني = mud/clay (variant of طين = clay, mud, silt). This is a different Arabic root from الطنء (GRC_0335). Greek τέναγος = shallow water, shoal, ford (often muddy/silty shallow areas). The semantic journey: mud/clay/silt -> the muddy shallow water environment where silt accumulates. A 1-step material-culture connection: river mud -> muddy shallows.
key_insight: Arabic mud/silt and Greek shallow muddy water are connected — silt is what defines a shoal/shallow ford.

semantic_journey: Arabic العرد = hard, stiff, rigid, difficult (of character or material). Greek ἐργώδης = laborious, troublesome, difficult to accomplish. The semantic journey: hard/stiff/rigid (material quality) -> laborious/difficult (what hard things produce when worked). Both encode HARDNESS/DIFFICULTY: Arabic as a physical quality, Greek as the experiential result of encountering something hard. A clean 1-step quality-to-experience mapping.
key_insight: Arabic hard/rigid (العرد) and Greek laborious/difficult (ἐργώδης) share the core of hardness — quality and its experiential consequence.

semantic_journey: Arabic العرزم = gathering, assembling (people or things together). Greek ἁρμόζω = to fit together, join, fasten (mechanical joining/fitting). The original reasoning maps gathering to assembly. But Arabic gathering is people coming together socially; Greek fitting is mechanical precision-joining of parts. These are different operations: social assembly vs mechanical fitting. The semantic path social gathering -> mechanical fitting requires intermediate steps that are not natural.
key_insight: Arabic social gathering and Greek mechanical fitting/joining are different operations — no direct 1-2 step path between them.

semantic_journey: Arabic العلق = bloodsucker/leech; also to cling, hang (علق = to hang/cling); also a clot of blood. Greek λάκκος = a pit, reservoir, cistern (for water). The original reasoning claims both relate to waterways. But Arabic العلق is a leech or blood-clot, not a water body. A leech lives in water, but that is not the word's meaning. λάκκος is a constructed water container. No genuine path.
key_insight: Arabic leech/blood-clot and Greek cistern/pit share only the incidental fact that leeches live in water — not a semantic path.

semantic_journey: Arabic حكا/حكى = to narrate, recount, tell a story, imitate/mimic. Greek καυχάομαι = to boast, brag, speak proudly of oneself. Both involve SPEECH ABOUT EVENTS OR DEEDS: Arabic = recounting/narrating (third-person storytelling); Greek = boasting/proclaiming one's own deeds (first-person). The step: narrating deeds -> boasting of deeds. A 1-step specialization where the neutral storytelling becomes self-promotional speech.
key_insight: Arabic narrating/recounting and Greek boasting/proclaiming share the core of verbal report of deeds — narration specializing into self-promotion.

semantic_journey: Arabic حكي = same root as GRC_0340, to narrate, tell, imitate. Greek καυχάομαι = to boast. Same analysis applies. Duplicate entry of the same root variant. Both encode verbal report of deeds moving toward boastful speech.
key_insight: Same as GRC_0340: Arabic narrating/imitating and Greek boasting share the verbal-report-of-deeds core.

semantic_journey: Arabic خبن = to fold cloth, tuck up, gather fabric (of garments); also refers to cloth processing actions. Greek κνάφος = a teasel or fuller's carding instrument used for processing cloth fiber. The semantic journey: folding/gathering cloth -> the instrument used in cloth-processing. Both belong to the TEXTILE CRAFT domain: Arabic = action performed on cloth, Greek = instrument for processing cloth fiber. A 1-step action-to-tool relationship within the same craft domain.
key_insight: Arabic cloth-folding/processing and Greek cloth-carding instrument both belong to textile craft — action and tool in the same domain.

semantic_journey: Arabic دلع = tongue sticking out, protruding tongue; also to be spoiled/pampered. Greek Δέλτα = the letter delta (Δ), fourth letter of Greek alphabet. The original reasoning claims the letter shape resembles a triangular tongue. But Δέλτα is a proper name (letter name) derived from the Semitic letter DALET (door). The shape of the letter comes from DALET (door), not from a tongue. Even if one accepted the shape argument, letter names are proper names of a different category. This is a false etymology.
key_insight: Greek Δέλτα derives from Semitic DALET (door), not from tongue-shape — this is a false etymology connecting the wrong Arabic concept.
